scripts.title: Why ads don't work (you're doing it wrong)
scripts.film: Sit down, relaxed, direct eye contact. Natural light. Lean in slightly when you get to the algorithm point — that's the key moment. Keep it like you're explaining this to someone across the table.

captions.label: A
captions.text: ads don't fail because they don't work. they fail because you quit before they had a chance to.

captions.label: B
captions.text: week one of meta ads is not performance. it's the algorithm learning your market. most service business owners kill the campaign right when it's about to actually work.

captions.label: C
captions.text: you're not buying leads in week one. you're buying data.

lines.text: You spent money on Meta ads and it didn't work. I can almost guarantee I know exactly why — and it has nothing to do with the ad itself.

The first one to two weeks of any Meta campaign is data collection. The algorithm doesn't know who your customer is yet. Every impression, every scroll, every click is feeding the machine so it can learn your market. That's what you're paying for — not leads.

So yes, week one looks rough. High cost per lead. Low volume. Feels like it's broken. Most service business owners see that and kill the campaign right there. But that's like pulling a cake out of the oven after five minutes and saying baking doesn't work.

The accounts I manage that are actually performing? Consistent spend for six to eight weeks minimum. Once the algorithm locks onto your ideal customer, your cost per lead drops fast and volume grows without touching anything. But you have to let it get there.
The ads weren't broken. The timeline was wrong.

scripts.title: Why some service businesses scale and others don't
scripts.film: Sit down, relaxed, natural lighting. This one is authoritative — you've seen this play out a hundred times. Steady delivery, not rushed. One hand gesture max when you hit the three points.

captions.label: A
captions.text: it's not budget. it's not the platform. the businesses scaling right now just do three things the stuck ones don't.

captions.label: B
captions.text: i manage ad accounts for service businesses across different trades. the ones scaling share one habit — they run ads consistently, track their numbers, and don't try to do it alone forever. the stuck ones do none of that.

captions.label: C
captions.text: turning ads on when slow and off when busy is why you never build momentum.

lines.text: I manage ad accounts for service businesses across different trades. The ones scaling right now have one thing in common — and it's not budget.

Consistency. The businesses stuck in the cycle of turning ads on when slow and off when busy will never build momentum. The algorithm resets every time. Real, predictable lead flow comes from always-on campaigns — not switching based on how full your calendar is this week.

They know their numbers. Cost per lead, close rate, cost per actual booked job. If you don't know what a customer costs you to acquire, you can't scale intelligently. You're just guessing. The businesses growing are making data-driven decisions every week.

They don't try to manage it themselves forever. At some point they bring in someone who specialises in this so they can focus on doing the work and growing their team. Running your own ads full-time is not a growth strategy.
Scaling isn't about spending more. It's about being consistent, knowing your numbers, and getting the right help.

scripts.title: The hook formula that actually works for service businesses
scripts.film: Sit down, casual, natural light. This one is direct and punchy — your energy should match. Short, sharp sentences. Look straight at camera for the close.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your service business ad loses people in the first 3 seconds because you're talking about yourself instead of their problem.

captions.label: B
captions.text: the hook that works isn't about what you do. it's about the exact situation your customer is already in. lead with that and they stop scrolling.

captions.label: C
captions.text: lead with the problem. everything else follows.

lines.text: Your service business ad is losing people in the first three seconds — and it's because you're talking about yourself when you should be talking about them.

Most service business ads open with what you do. 'We're your local plumber.' 'Professional window cleaning.' But your customer doesn't care about that yet. They care about their problem. 'Burst pipe at 11pm? We answer.' That's a hook. The second one triggers recognition. The first is just noise.

Specificity beats everything. 'Dirty gutters before the rainy season' beats 'professional gutter cleaning service' every single time. Real situations that people are actually living. That's what stops a scroll and makes someone think — that's me, I need to watch this.

Once you have their attention with a problem they already recognise, everything else follows. The solution, the offer, the call to action — they actually listen because you earned it in the first three seconds. Miss those three seconds and the rest doesn't matter.
Three seconds to grab them. Lead with their problem every single time.

scripts.title: Real job photos beat stock images every time
scripts.film: Sit down or stand, relaxed workspace, natural light. When you get to point 2 about shooting on your phone — hold yours up briefly. Keeps it real and visual. Energy is genuine, not hype.

captions.label: A
captions.text: swapped a client's stock photos for real job site photos last week. their cost per click dropped by more than half.

captions.label: B
captions.text: people can smell stock photography on a service business ad from a mile away. real before-and-afters, real job sites, real dirty hands — that's what builds trust and gets the click.

captions.label: C
captions.text: your competitors are all running the same five stock photos. you don't have to.

lines.text: I swapped a client's stock photo ads for photos their crew took on-site last week. Cost per click dropped by more than half. That is not luck — that is trust.

People scrolling Facebook know a stock image the second they see one. It screams generic. But when they see an actual before-and-after from a real job in their area — a real bathroom renovation, a real driveway cleaned, a real gutter cleared — something clicks. They think: that could be my house.

You don't need a photographer. You need the habit. Take your phone out on every job. Shoot the before. Shoot the during. Shoot the finished result. That's your entire creative library — completely free and updating every day you work.

Your competitors are all running the same five contractor stock photos. You're running real work that proves you actually do this. The gap in trust shows up directly in your click-through rate and your cost per lead. This is one of the easiest wins in your whole ad account.
Your next client could come from a photo you take on your next job. Start shooting.

scripts.title: Why your ads die after 3 days
scripts.film: Sit down, direct to camera, relaxed. Keep it punchy and matter-of-fact — like you're explaining something obvious that most people just don't know yet. Short sentences, clear delivery.

captions.label: A
captions.text: if your ads spike and die every 3-4 days, you don't have a budget problem. you have an audience size problem.

captions.label: B
captions.text: ad fatigue is real and it's preventable. check your frequency in ads manager. if it's above 2.5 in the first week, you're burning through your audience faster than you can replenish it.

captions.label: C
captions.text: ads don't die. audiences get exhausted. know the difference.

lines.text: If your ads spike and die every three to four days, you don't have a budget problem. You have an audience size problem.

Meta shows your ad to the same pool of people. Small audience, your frequency climbs fast. They've seen your ad four, five, six times. The response rate collapses. This is ad fatigue — and it's one of the most common reasons service business campaigns burn out fast.

Go check your frequency in Ads Manager right now. If it's above 2.5 on a campaign that's only been running a week, you're burning through your audience faster than you're replacing it. Your cost per lead goes up. Conversion rate drops. And you think the ad stopped working — but it's the audience that's exhausted.

Two fixes: broaden your targeting so you're reaching more new people, or rotate fresh creative every few days. Different hook, different angle, same offer. Keep it fresh and Meta keeps delivering it effectively.
Ads don't die. Audiences get exhausted. Know the difference and you'll stop wasting budget.

scripts.title: The ROI Math Behind a Profitable Meta Campaign
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHAalyp4=/. Walk through each step in the funnel with your cursor — pause on each one so viewers can read it. Speak naturally as you point through REACH, CLICK, LEAD, CLIENT. Let the board do the visual work.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service business owners are losing money at one specific step in their funnel. the ad is almost never the problem.

captions.label: B
captions.text: your meta campaign is four steps: reach, click, lead, client. most service businesses bleed money between steps 2 and 3 — not because the ad is bad, but because what happens after the click is broken.

captions.label: C
captions.text: fix one step in this chain and your whole return improves without spending more.

lines.text: Walk through this with me — most service business owners are losing money at one specific step in their ad funnel, and they don't know which one.

Step one — Reach. Your ad gets in front of the right people. If targeting is too broad, you're paying to reach people who'd never hire you. That's where the waste starts — before anyone even sees your creative.

Step two — Click. A strong hook earns the click. Weak creative loses them right here. Step three — Lead. Your landing page or offer converts that click into a real inquiry. This is where most service businesses actually bleed money. A good ad sending people to a weak page is just expensive traffic.

Step four — Client. Your follow-up speed and how you present converts that lead into a paying job. Most owners pour all their focus into the ad and ignore everything after it. Fix step three or four and your return improves without touching the campaign.
Find the leak. Fix it. Most of the time it's not the ad.

scripts.title: Why Your Ad Spend Feels Wasted (And How to Fix It)
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHAbICr4=/. Walk through each mistake circle with your cursor, pause so viewers can read it, then move to the fix. Let the board carry the visual — your voice is narrating what they're seeing.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your ad spend isn't the problem. these three things are.

captions.label: B
captions.text: if your meta ads feel like burning money, it's almost always wrong audience, weak creative, or no follow-up system. fix the biggest leak first and watch what changes.

captions.label: C
captions.text: ads don't fail. the system around them does.

lines.text: Three reasons your Meta ad spend feels like burning money — and none of them are the platform's fault.

Wrong audience. You're paying to reach people who will never buy from you. A window cleaner targeting everyone aged 18 to 65 in their city is paying for people in apartments with no windows to clean. Get specific — homeowners, the right suburbs, the right demographics. Tighter targeting means every dollar works harder.

Weak creative. The ad doesn't stop the scroll. No attention means no click means no lead. This is almost always a hook problem — the first two seconds aren't speaking to anyone's specific pain. It's generic. It gets ignored.

No follow-up system. This is the one nobody talks about. Your ad is actually working — leads are coming in — but they go cold because no one follows up fast enough. A lead that waits 24 hours for a call back is a lead you've already lost to your competitor.
Fix the biggest leak first. Targeting, creative, or follow-up — find your one and start there.

scripts.title: Why service owners quit ads too early
scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, steady energy. This one is authoritative — you've seen this exact thing happen over and over. Lean in slightly on the algorithm point. Deliver it like you're giving them advice they needed six months ago.

captions.label: A
captions.text: every business owner who tells me meta ads don't work quit between week 2 and week 4. that's not a coincidence.

captions.label: B
captions.text: weeks 1-2 of meta ads are the learning phase. the algorithm is still finding your customer. high lead cost at this stage is normal — it's the machine working, not failing. most owners panic and pull the plug right before the breakthrough.

captions.label: C
captions.text: set a minimum of 6 weeks before you judge. that's how the system actually works.

lines.text: Every single business owner I've talked to who says Meta ads don't work quit somewhere between week two and week four. That is not a coincidence.

Weeks one and two are the learning phase. Meta's algorithm is collecting data — it doesn't know your ideal customer yet. High cost per lead, lower volume. That is not failure. That is the machine figuring out your market. Your only job in those two weeks is to not touch anything.

By week four or five, the algorithm has found your buyers. Cost per lead drops. Volume picks up. The businesses seeing real results are the ones who committed past that uncomfortable first fortnight and let it run.

The ones who quit made a decision based on week-one data. That's like cancelling your gym membership after the first sore workout and deciding fitness doesn't work. You pulled it before it had a chance to prove itself.
Set a minimum — six weeks of consistent spend before you judge. That's not hope. That's just how the system works.

scripts.title: The campaign objective mistake costing you leads
scripts.film: Sit down, direct to camera, office or home setup. Lean in when you explain the switch from traffic to conversions — that's the moment that lands. Confident, not rushed.

captions.label: A
captions.text: you're getting clicks. your phone isn't ringing. your campaign objective is probably why.

captions.label: B
captions.text: traffic campaigns find people who click. conversion campaigns find people who call and book. same ad, same budget — completely different quality of lead depending on which one you pick.

captions.label: C
captions.text: one setting change can completely change your results. most service businesses never make it.

lines.text: You're getting clicks. Your phone isn't ringing. Your campaign objective is probably why.

Most service businesses pick Traffic or Reach as their objective. That tells Meta to find people who click — not people who call, book, or send enquiries. So the algorithm optimises for cheap clicks and delivers low-intent traffic. Lots of activity, barely any leads.

Switch to Conversions and tie it to actual phone call data or booking confirmations. Now Meta knows what a real result looks like for your business, and it will go find more of those people. Completely different quality of traffic — same creative, same budget.

I've switched businesses from traffic campaigns to conversion campaigns and watched cost per actual lead drop significantly — just from changing that one setting. The creative didn't change. The offer didn't change. The objective changed.
Same spend, better outcomes. Tell the algorithm what you actually want.

scripts.title: Why your lead cost keeps climbing

overlay.line2: it's one of these three things

scripts.film: Sit down, natural light, relaxed but direct. You're diagnosing a problem here — deliver it like a clear, structured breakdown. Pause slightly between each of the three points so it lands.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your lead cost isn't high because of meta. it's high because of one of three fixable things.

captions.label: B
captions.text: rising lead cost is almost always targeting that's too broad, a landing page that isn't converting, or unqualified people filling your form. lead cost is a symptom. find which one is leaking.

captions.label: C
captions.text: lead cost is a symptom. the real problem is fixable.

lines.text: If your lead cost is going up over time, one of three things is wrong — and only one of them has anything to do with the platform.

Targeting too broad. You're paying to reach people who would never hire you. A plumber reaching renters. An HVAC business reaching people who don't own their home. Tighter targeting means less wasted impressions and better signal for the algorithm. Tighten it up and watch your cost shift.

Your landing page isn't converting. Clicks are coming but no one's filling out the form. That is not an ad problem — that is a page problem. Slow load time, confusing offer, no social proof. Fix the page and your lead cost drops without touching the campaign.

You're not pre-qualifying. Every junk submission costs you money. Add one filter question to your form — what's the issue, what's your timeline, what suburb are you in. Fewer leads, but better leads, and lower effective cost per actual job.
Lead cost is a symptom. Find which one of those three is leaking and fix that first.

scripts.title: Structure your Meta campaign right from the start
scripts.film: Sit down, well-lit, direct to camera. Calm and structured delivery — this is a three-point explainer. Light hand gestures to emphasise each point. Confident, not lecture-y.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service businesses skip the foundation and wonder why their ads don't convert. structure matters more than budget.

captions.label: B
captions.text: three things the best-performing service business campaigns have: cold and warm audiences separated, multiple creatives being tested, and attribution tied to actual booked jobs — not just clicks.

captions.label: C
captions.text: get the structure right first. the money follows.

lines.text: The structure of your Meta campaign matters more than your budget — and most service businesses get this wrong from day one.

Separate your cold and warm audiences from the start. Cold traffic — people who've never heard of you — needs different messaging than warm traffic — people who visited your site or engaged with your page. Running one campaign to both is like using the exact same pitch on a stranger and a repeat customer.

Test multiple creatives from the beginning. At least three different angles — real job results, a problem-focused hook, a before-and-after. Let Meta figure out which one resonates. You'll know within seven to ten days. Then you scale what works and cut what doesn't.

Track what leads to actual booked jobs, not just clicks and form fills. Build the attribution chain. Which campaign, which ad, which audience produced the work that actually made money. Everything else is noise until you know that number.
Get the foundation right. Everything that comes after gets easier and cheaper.

scripts.title: Boosting posts is not running ads
scripts.film: Stand or sit, relaxed, direct energy. This one can be a bit sharper in delivery — like you're correcting a common misconception you've heard a hundred times. Brief phone in hand if it helps with the 'boost button' reference.

captions.label: A
captions.text: boosting posts is not running ads. i know it feels like ads. it's not.

captions.label: B
captions.text: boosted posts reach your existing audience. real campaigns find completely new customers based on intent and behaviour. if you're spending money on ads to grow your business — not just your page — you need to be in ads manager.

captions.label: C
captions.text: use boost for reach. use proper campaigns for revenue.

lines.text: Boosting posts is not running ads. I know it feels like ads. It is not.

When you boost a post, Meta shows it to your existing followers and people similar to them. You're basically paying to reach people who already know you exist. It's cheap, it's fast, it feels like you're doing advertising — but you're mostly talking to the same room.

A real campaign in Ads Manager lets you build custom audiences based on actual intent signals. People who visited your website, people in your target suburbs, homeowners in the right income bracket. You're targeting the right person, at the right time, with the right message — not just boosting content at your existing page likes.

The results are completely different. Boosted posts get engagement. Real campaigns get phone calls and booked jobs. If your goal is to grow your business — not just get likes — you need to be building proper campaigns with real targeting and conversion tracking.
Boost for brand awareness. Build real campaigns for actual revenue. Know which one you're running.

scripts.title: What Happens in the First 30 Days of Meta Ads
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHAboYFU=/. Walk through each phase with your cursor, pausing so viewers can read each arrow. Let the phases do the visual work — your voice narrates what's happening in each one.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most business owners quit at day 7. here's what the full 30-day picture actually looks like.

captions.label: B
captions.text: the first 30 days of meta ads have four distinct phases — learning, data, scale, refine. most service businesses never get past phase one because they panic at the numbers and turn it off. here's what's supposed to happen.

captions.label: C
captions.text: you don't get results in week one. you get data. the results come later.

lines.text: Most service business owners quit Meta ads at day 7 wondering why it isn't working. Here's what's actually supposed to happen across those 30 days.

Days 1 to 7 — the learning phase. Meta's algorithm is figuring out who responds to your ad. Your only job is to not touch anything. Changing settings, pausing, adjusting budget — all of it resets the learning phase. Just let it run.

Days 8 to 14 — first real data. You can see what's working. Make small adjustments if needed. Days 15 to 21 — you've found what converts. Now you scale it slowly. Increase budget by no more than 20 percent every few days. Cut what isn't converting.

Days 22 to 30 — leads are coming in consistently. Now you refine. Tighter audiences, stronger creative, lower cost per lead. This is where the real momentum starts building and your results compound week over week.
By day 30 you know exactly what works in your market. The businesses with real results are the ones who committed past that uncomfortable first week.

scripts.title: Why retargeting is your cheapest lead source
scripts.film: Screen record Meta Ads Manager. Start on a campaign dashboard. Navigate to show a retargeting audience vs a cold traffic audience — highlight the size difference. Then move to Results and compare cost per lead between a cold campaign and a retargeting campaign side by side. Keep cursor intentional, zoom in on the key numbers, speak naturally over it.

captions.label: A
captions.text: if you're spending all your budget on cold traffic, you're leaving your cheapest leads sitting there untouched.

captions.label: B
captions.text: retargeting works because they've already seen your business. they just need one more reason to call. that costs less than convincing a stranger from scratch — every single time.

captions.label: C
captions.text: cheapest leads you'll ever pay for are the ones who already know you exist.

lines.text: If you're spending all your budget on cold traffic and barely running retargeting, you're doing it backwards.

Look at this — cold traffic campaign. New people who've never heard of your business. Cost per lead is solid, but you're working hard to convince a stranger. They don't know your work, they don't trust you yet. Meta has to show the ad multiple times before anyone acts.

Now look at retargeting. Same business, same offer. But these are people who already visited your website, saw your work, maybe even filled in a form and didn't book. They're warm. The conversion intent is already there — you're not convincing anymore, you're just reminding.

Cost per lead is consistently lower from retargeting. You're paying to close, not to introduce. And the quality of those leads is higher because these people already chose to learn more about you. That's where your ad budget works hardest.
Run cold traffic to fill the top of your funnel. Run retargeting to convert what's already there. Both together is how you build a real lead system.

captions.label: C
captions.text: fix one step in this chain and your whole return improves without spending more.

lines.text: Walk through this with me — most service business owners are losing money at one specific step in their ad funnel, and they don't know which one.

Step one — Reach. Your ad gets in front of the right people. If targeting is too broad, you're paying to reach people who'd never hire you. That's where the waste starts — before anyone even sees your creative.

Step two — Click. A strong hook earns the click. Weak creative loses them right here. Step three — Lead. Your landing page or offer converts that click into a real inquiry. This is where most service businesses actually bleed money. A good ad sending people to a weak page is just expensive traffic.

Step four — Client. Your follow-up speed and how you present converts that lead into a paying job. Most owners pour all their focus into the ad and ignore everything after it. Fix step three or four and your return improves without touching the campaign.
Find the leak. Fix it. Most of the time it's not the ad.

scripts.title: Why Your Ad Spend Feels Wasted (And How to Fix It)
scripts.film: Screen record the Miro board at https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVHAbICr4=/. Walk through each mistake circle with your cursor, pause so viewers can read it, then move to the fix. Let the board carry the visual — your voice is narrating what they're seeing.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your ad spend isn't the problem. these three things are.

captions.label: B
captions.text: if your meta ads feel like burning money, it's almost always wrong audience, weak creative, or no follow-up system. fix the biggest leak first and watch what changes.

captions.label: C
captions.text: ads don't fail. the system around them does.

lines.text: Three reasons your Meta ad spend feels like burning money — and none of them are the platform's fault.

Wrong audience. You're paying to reach people who will never buy from you. A window cleaner targeting everyone aged 18 to 65 in their city is paying for people in apartments with no windows to clean. Get specific — homeowners, the right suburbs, the right demographics. Tighter targeting means every dollar works harder.

Weak creative. The ad doesn't stop the scroll. No attention means no click means no lead. This is almost always a hook problem — the first two seconds aren't speaking to anyone's specific pain. It's generic. It gets ignored.

No follow-up system. This is the one nobody talks about. Your ad is actually working — leads are coming in — but they go cold because no one follows up fast enough. A lead that waits 24 hours for a call back is a lead you've already lost to your competitor.
Fix the biggest leak first. Targeting, creative, or follow-up — find your one and start there.
